# RFC-0002: Workspace And Serving Environment Contract

> **Version:** 0.3.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0002:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0002c-summary"></a>

This RFC governs the workspace and its serving environments: what one workspace revision fixes and excludes (authority and source identity), the local-bindings file that supplies machine-private facts, dirty-workspace execution policy, the committed Pixi environment lifecycle from clean lock production to locked installation, declared environment realization checks, and per-process runtime JIT cache allocation. [RFC-0001](../rfc/RFC-0001.md) holds the specification overview and the authority map.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0002:C-WORKSPACE-AUTHORITY] Workspace And Local Authority (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-workspace-authority"></a>

A shareable execution baseline MUST be identified by one workspace revision that fixes the relevant source tree and submodule revisions, Pixi serving manifests and locks, framework integration package selection, reusable definitions, and recipes.

Ordinary execution of a recipe MUST NOT require the operator to restate a model, serve profile, source set, environment, measurement definition, provider location, or framework integration version already selected by the workspace and recipe.

A workspace MAY organize reusable definitions across multiple files. File organization MUST NOT create implicit behavior-affecting precedence between equivalent definitions.

Multi-file organization MUST compose the root `.inferlab/workspace.toml` with fragments under `.inferlab/workspace.d/*.toml` as a disjoint union of identifier-keyed definitions.

An identifier declared by two workspace files MUST be rejected at load with an error naming both files.

`schema_version` and any workspace-global scalar MUST appear only in the root workspace file; a fragment that declares one MUST be rejected at load naming the fragment.

Every path component of the workspace configuration files — the `.inferlab` directory, the root workspace file, the `workspace.d` directory, and every workspace fragment — MUST be a non-symbolic-link filesystem entry; a symbolic-link component MUST be rejected at load, because the workspace source digest records link text rather than target content.

Every component of each declared source-set path below the workspace root MUST likewise be a non-symbolic-link entry, rejected at load for the same reason.

Every symbolic link effectively present in the digested worktree — below the workspace root, outside every `.git` entry, outside the workspace source exclusions (the local-bindings file and the `.inferlab` `cache`, `records`, `runtime`, and `scratchpads` subtrees), and outside git-ignored directories, which the walk does not enter — MUST resolve within the workspace root and outside every `.git` entry. A digest-visible link — one the source digest records: tracked, or untracked and not git-ignored, including a link replacing a tracked entry — MUST further carry a relative target that lexically resolves to identity-covered content: not under a workspace source exclusion and not content git-ignored as judged by the target's owning repository. A target that lexically resolves onto or through another digest-visible link is judged against its link-resolved destination, substituting digest-visible link text until no digest-visible link component remains; a digest-visible target that resolves onto or through a machine-local link MUST be rejected naming the starting link, its target, and the machine-local link, because the machine-local link's text is outside the recorded identity and its retarget would change effective content under an unchanged digest; a substitution chain that revisits a link is a cycle and MUST be rejected naming the starting link and its target. A violating link MUST be rejected when the workspace snapshot claims source identity, naming the link path and the link target — and the declaring source set when the link lies under one — because the source digest records link text rather than target content: a target outside the claimed identity lets the bytes frameworks actually read drift without changing any recorded identity or runtime-cache namespace.

A git-ignored link is machine-local state no identity claim covers — real editable installs plant absolute links to in-root content — so containment alone binds it, and an absolute target is judged by whether it resolves under this workspace's root. A git-ignored directory is a machine-local tree outside the digested worktree: the walk neither enters it nor judges its contents, because the digest cannot see them and a digest-visible link targeting git-ignored content is already rejected, so nothing inside one can ride the identity claim. A digest-visible link whose target is identity-covered remains permitted, as is a dangling internal target, which the link text alone identifies. Tracking state otherwise affects dirtiness, not containment; resolution is lexical for relative targets, and every intermediate link is enumerated and judged on its own by construction, because the walk covers the whole digested worktree rather than the declared source-set subtrees.

Local bindings MUST remain separate from shareable workspace content.

Inferlab MUST discover local bindings from `.inferlab/local.toml` under the workspace root by default.

An operator MUST be able to select an alternate local-bindings file explicitly with `--local <file>`.

Local bindings MAY supply model-weight locators, machines, devices, launch access, credentials, builders, caches, artifact locations, and other machine-private facts.

Local bindings MUST NOT contain persistent overrides for behavior-affecting recipe, model, serve-profile, source, environment, Eval, Bench, or image settings.

Long-running server and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST permit execution from a dirty workspace for local development.

Dirty-workspace resolution MUST identify the baseline revision, the effective dirty source state by content digest, and that revision-only reproduction is not possible.

The workspace serving closure MUST NOT include lm-eval, AIPerf, an Inferlab Eval or Bench runner, or an Eval-only or Bench-only Pixi environment.

The Inferlab-owned measurement toolchains ([RFC-0004:C-INFERLAB-TOOLCHAIN](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-inferlab-toolchain)) MUST be fixed by the installed Inferlab release and remain outside workspace package authority; the workspace-side Python packages are inside it ([RFC-0006:C-INTEGRATIONS](../rfc/RFC-0006.md#rfc-0006c-integrations)).

**Rationale:** Git and Pixi provide the serving baseline shared by collaborators, while a conventional local file supplies private machine facts without repeated CLI wiring. Allowing explicitly recorded dirty execution preserves the fast edit-and-run loop required for inference optimization without misrepresenting that run as reproducible from the baseline revision alone. Keeping generic measurement tooling release-owned prevents it from perturbing framework dependency resolution or being repeated in every experiment workspace. The containment walk covers the digested worktree rather than the declared source-set subtrees because the source digest pathspec covers the root: a link outside every source set still enters identity as link text, and a root-level bridge link outside the walked subtrees would escape in two hops — a source-set link resolving onto it passes containment while the bridge carries an absolute external target that no judgment ever examines. Ignore judgment runs on link-resolved destinations because a candidate path crossing a link component is otherwise unjudgeable — git refuses pathspecs beyond a symbolic link — and a benign in-root chain through a link directory must not fail on tool mechanics. Git-ignored directories stay out of the walk because a machine-local tree such as a package environment may legitimately hold thousands of links whose judgment buys no integrity.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-PIXI-ENVIRONMENT-LIFECYCLE] Pixi Environment Lifecycle (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-pixi-environment-lifecycle"></a>

A workspace serving environment MUST be defined by the committed Pixi manifest and lock selected by the workspace revision.

The public CLI MUST provide an `env lock` operation that can produce the full workspace serving lock from a clean local package prefix.

The `env lock` operation MUST NOT require an operator to perform manual intermediate manifest edits or out-of-band package installation.

Successful lock production MUST preserve the authoritative workspace manifest byte-for-byte and leave the resulting full lock as the workspace lock.

After a handled failure or interruption, lock production MUST restore the authoritative workspace manifest and any pre-existing workspace lock.

Intermediate package state used while producing a lock MUST NOT become a second workspace manifest, lock, environment identity, or experiment input.

Ordinary workspace setup MUST install the selected serving environment from the committed lock without updating that lock or the authoritative manifest.

Dry-run, long-running server, and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST activate the already installed selected serving environment without installing packages or updating the workspace manifest or lock, unless a runtime image substitutes for the locally installed serving environment ([RFC-0003:C-RUNTIME-WORKFLOWS](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-runtime-workflows)) — the image is then the serving environment and no local installation is consulted.

When no image substitutes and the selected serving environment is absent or not usable with the committed lock, those workflows MUST fail before server launch or measurement execution with an error that identifies the environment and the locked installation action required.

Before launching any server process, long-running server and closed-loop recipe workflows MUST execute the declared environment checks of the selected serving environment against each distinct machine realization the launch consumes — the controller's installed environment and each remote machine's ([RFC-0002:C-ENVIRONMENT-CHECKS](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-environment-checks)); a failed check MUST fail before any process launches and present the declared repair hint. When a built image substitutes for the locally installed serving environment, its realization was already checked during image assembly and this preflight does not apply; when a declared external image substitutes, the launch is not qualified by this workspace and records no environment-check claim ([RFC-0003:C-RUNTIME-WORKFLOWS](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-runtime-workflows)), so this preflight likewise does not apply.

Experiment execution MUST NOT create a recipe-specific or source-set-specific Pixi manifest, lock, or materialized environment closure.

Dry-run output and execution records MUST identify the selected Pixi serving environment and the committed manifest and lock digests.

The workspace serving environment lifecycle is independent of the Inferlab-owned measurement-toolchain lifecycle defined by [RFC-0004:C-INFERLAB-TOOLCHAIN](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-inferlab-toolchain).

**Rationale:** A committed lock gives collaborators a simple fixed-workspace installation model. Automated clean lock production removes the hidden dependency on an already-warm package prefix, while separating lock production, locked installation, and no-sync execution keeps a dynamic environment system out of the contract. A separate built-in measurement toolchain prevents generic Eval and Bench dependencies from entering this serving closure. Executing declared realization checks on every machine the launch consumes prices environment drift at seconds before GPU time is committed, and keeps repair with the operator who owns each drifted environment.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-ENVIRONMENT-CHECKS] Environment Realization Checks (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-environment-checks"></a>

A workspace environment definition MAY declare environment realization checks and image-realization postprocess steps. Both are committed workspace content: the workspace owns the knowledge of what its environment must satisfy, while Inferlab owns only the execution contract.

The committed Pixi lock is the single authority for environment content, but each installed instance of that lock — the local workspace environment, each remote machine's workspace environment, an image environment — is a distinct realization produced by installer tooling whose behavior the lock does not fully determine. Checks assert workspace-declared invariants against a realization.

A check MUST be a workspace-relative Python script executed with the selected environment's Python interpreter. Exit status zero is the sole pass signal. A check MUST NOT mutate the realization it examines. Check output MUST report facts, not remedies; a check declaration MAY carry a repair hint for the operator.

One declared check set MUST serve every realization point. This specification requires execution at image-build entry against the local workspace realization and inside image assembly against the image realization ([RFC-0007:C-IMAGE-BUILD](../rfc/RFC-0007.md#rfc-0007c-image-build)), and before server launch against each distinct machine realization the launch consumes — the controller's installed environment and each remote machine's ([RFC-0002:C-PIXI-ENVIRONMENT-LIFECYCLE](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-pixi-environment-lifecycle)).

A failed check MUST fail the consuming workflow; there is no warning level. Repair responsibility follows realization ownership. The operator owns workspace realizations: a check failure there means the realization drifted, the failure MUST present the declared repair hint, and Inferlab MUST NOT mutate the environment itself. The build owns the image realization: a check failure there means a systematic defect in the build inputs or procedure, the failure MUST NOT present the repair hint, and the remedy is correcting the inputs and rebuilding.

An image-realization postprocess step MUST be a workspace-relative Python script executed only during image assembly, after environment installation and before the checks, as a deterministic finishing step of that realization — its effect MUST derive only from the frozen build context and the environment's locked closure. Postprocess steps MUST NOT execute against a workspace environment.

Every executed check MUST be recorded with the realization it examined — including which machine, where realizations are machine-scoped — its outcome, and its captured output, in the record of the consuming workflow. A check execution failure that is infrastructural rather than a check verdict MUST still finalize the consuming record.

**Rationale:** Realization drift is real, realization-specific, and cheapest to catch early: the same lock has produced a broken host environment and a working image at once, and a defect detectable on the host in seconds has surfaced only after an hour of compilation. One declared invariant set with per-realization execution keeps a single authority for what the environment must satisfy, makes the failure site diagnostic — a workspace failure means drift, an image failure means a systematic input defect — and sizes repair to who can act: the operator repairs mutable workspace environments, the build finishes the image realization deterministically because no one can repair an immutable artifact. Remote machines install the same lock independently, so they drift independently; checking only the controller would launch ranks into exactly the drift this layer exists to catch.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-RUNTIME-CACHE] Runtime JIT Cache Allocation (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-runtime-cache"></a>

Before final process rendering, Inferlab MUST allocate one runtime JIT cache root for every planned server process.

A local machine binding MAY provide the storage root from which that process cache is allocated.

When a local machine binding does not provide a cache storage root, Inferlab MUST use deterministic workspace-local state as the storage root.

A runtime JIT cache namespace MUST include the resolved workspace source state, selected Pixi environment identity, concrete machine identity, and process identity. When an image substitutes for the locally installed serving environment — a built image or a declared external image — the namespace MUST key on the image's immutable identity in place of the invoking workspace source state and Pixi environment identity: the image, not the invoking checkout, is the software identity that generates and consumes the cached artifacts.

Processes assigned to different machines or different process identities MUST NOT receive the same mutable runtime JIT cache path, even when their storage roots refer to one shared filesystem.

The selected framework integration MUST receive the allocated process cache root when rendering the final invocation.

Framework-specific cache families, subdirectories, and environment-variable names MUST remain owned by the selected framework integration rather than the framework-neutral Rust control plane.

An explicit framework environment override MAY replace an integration-provided cache environment default, and the effective value MUST remain visible in dry-run output and execution records.

Real execution MUST create the allocated cache root on the target machine before launching the framework process.

Dry-run output and execution records MUST preserve each process cache storage root, namespace inputs, allocated path, and effective framework cache environment values.

**Rationale:** Runtime-generated compiler and kernel state is required by real inference frameworks but is not safe to leave implicitly under a shared home directory. Per-process allocation isolates runtime-generated state without introducing general cache-policy or dependency-projection machinery. Keying an image-backed namespace on the image identity keeps one software identity per mutable cache path — an image qualified at one revision must never share JIT artifacts with the invoking checkout's own environment. Keeping framework variable mapping in integrations lets that mapping evolve with vLLM, SGLang, and related tools while Rust retains path allocation and evidence authority.

*Since: v0.1.0*
